The patch clamp (Neher & Sakmann, 1976) extends this to single ion channels: a fire-polished pipette forms a gigaohm seal with the membrane, and current through individual channels is resolved in real time. Four patch-clamp configurations (cell-attached, whole-cell, inside-out, outside-out) allow study of single-channel and whole-cell currents under controlled intracellular and extracellular ionic conditions.
entries.note: Voltage clamp: Cole, K. S. (1949). 'Dynamic electrical characteristics of the squid axon membrane.' Archives des Sciences Physiologiques, 3, 253–258 — the technical invention. Marmont, G. (1949). 'Studies on the axon membrane.' Journal of Cellular and Comparative Physiology, 34(3), 351–382 — further development. Hodgkin & Huxley applied it in their four-paper series (1952). Patch clamp: Neher, E. & Sakmann, B. (1976). 'Single-channel currents recorded from membrane of denervated frog muscle fibres.' Nature, 260, 799–802. Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1991 (Neher & Sakmann). Gigaohm seal: Hamill, O. P. et al. (1981). 'Improved patch-clamp techniques for high-resolution current recording from cells and cell-free membrane patches.' Pflügers Archiv, 391(2), 85–100 — the technical paper establishing the standard configurations. Automated patch clamp: Milligan, C. J. et al. (2009). Nature Protocols, 4, 244–255 — high-throughput robotic patch platforms for ion channel pharmacology.

entries.label: Cardiac Catheterisation and Pressure-Volume Analysis
entries.hint: Cardiac catheterisation inserts a pressure-transducing catheter into heart chambers via peripheral vessels, enabling direct measurement of pressures in each chamber and across valves, and calculation of cardiac output by the Fick principle (CO = O₂ consumption ÷ arteriovenous O₂ difference) or thermodilution. Pressure-volume (PV) loops — plotting ventricular pressure against instantaneous volume through one cardiac cycle — are the gold standard for quantifying preload-independent contractility (end-systolic PV relationship, ESPVR), diastolic compliance (EDPVR), and external mechanical work (area of the PV loop). Measurement requires a conductance catheter (electrical admittance-based volume) combined with high-fidelity pressure transduction.
entries.note: Self-catheterisation: Forssmann, W. (1929). 'Die Sondierung des rechten Herzens.' Klinische Wochenschrift, 8(45), 2085–2087 — Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1956 (shared with Cournand and Richards). Cournand, A. & Ranges, H. A. (1941). 'Catheterization of the right auricle in man.' Proceedings of the Society for Experimental Biology and Medicine, 46(3), 462–466 — established right-heart catheterisation as a clinical tool. PV loops: Suga, H. & Sagawa, K. (1974). 'Instantaneous pressure-volume relationships and their ratio in the excised, supported canine left ventricle.' Circulation Research, 35(1), 117–126 — defined ESPVR and pressure-volume area. Swan, H. J. C. et al. (1970). 'Catheterization of the heart in man with use of a flow-directed balloon-tipped catheter.' New England Journal of Medicine, 283(9), 447–451 — the pulmonary artery (Swan-Ganz) catheter for bedside haemodynamic monitoring; clinical use has declined since RCTs showed no benefit and potential harm in general ICU patients (Sandham, J. D. et al. 2003. NEJM, 348, 5–14).

entries.label: Spirometry and Body Plethysmography
entries.hint: Spirometry measures volumes and flow rates of air during defined breathing manoeuvres. Key indices: FVC (forced vital capacity), FEV₁ (forced expired volume in 1 second), and FEV₁/FVC — below 0.70 indicating obstruction (asthma, COPD) and preserved but with proportionally reduced FVC indicating restriction. The flow-volume loop shape (concave expiratory limb in obstruction, symmetric but small loop in restriction) adds pattern recognition. Body plethysmography encloses the subject in an airtight box and applies Boyle's law to measure functional residual capacity, residual volume, and total lung capacity — volumes spirometry cannot access.
entries.note: Hutchinson, J. (1846). 'On the capacity of the lungs, and on the respiratory functions, with a view of establishing a precise and easy method of detecting disease by the spirometer.' Medico-Chirurgical Transactions, 29, 137–252 — invented the spirometer and measured vital capacity in 2,000 men, noting its decline with disease. FEV₁: Tiffeneau, R. & Pinelli, A. (1947). 'Air circulant et air captif dans l'exploration de la fonction ventilatoire pulmonaire.' Paris Médicale, 133, 624–628 — the FEV₁ concept. Body plethysmography: DuBois, A. B. et al. (1956). 'A rapid plethysmographic method for measuring thoracic gas volume.' Journal of Clinical Investigation, 35(3), 322–326. ATS/ERS standardisation: Miller, M. R. et al. (2005). European Respiratory Journal, 26(2), 319–338. Limitation: spirometry requires effort-dependent manoeuvres; reference values carry systematic biases from the populations used to derive them — the most widely used GLI-2012 equations (European cohorts) may not apply equally across all ethnicities.

entries.label: Renal Clearance Methods
entries.loc: New York University School of Medicine, New York City, New York
entries.hint: Renal clearance (C = U × V̇ / P, where U is urinary concentration, V̇ is urine flow, and P is plasma concentration) is the volume of plasma completely cleared of a substance per minute. Inulin clearance equals GFR because inulin is freely filtered and neither secreted nor reabsorbed. Para-aminohippurate (PAH) clearance at low plasma concentrations equals effective renal plasma flow because it is almost completely extracted in one pass. Together, inulin and PAH clearance define the filtered fraction and allow calculation of net tubular secretion or reabsorption for any other substance by comparing its clearance to inulin clearance.
entries.note: Smith, H. W., Goldring, W. & Chasis, H. (1938). 'The measurement of the tubular excretory mass, effective blood flow and filtration rate in the normal human kidney.' Journal of Clinical Investigation, 17(3), 263–278 — standardised inulin and PAH clearance methods in humans. Smith, H. W. (1956). Principles of Renal Physiology. Oxford University Press. Clinical approximations: creatinine clearance slightly overestimates GFR because creatinine is secreted by the proximal tubule; in advanced CKD this secretory fraction can represent 40–50% of urinary creatinine, causing serious overestimation. CKD-EPI eGFR equations (Levey, A. S. et al. 2009. Annals of Internal Medicine, 150(9), 604–612) correct for demographic factors. Cystatin C-based eGFR equations (Inker, L. A. et al. 2012. NEJM, 367, 20–29) are less affected by muscle mass variations and are increasingly recommended for diagnostic clarity.

entries.label: Radioimmunoassay and Immunoassay
entries.loc: Veterans Administration Hospital, Bronx, New York
entries.hint: Radioimmunoassay (RIA) uses competitive binding between radiolabelled antigen and unlabelled sample antigen for a fixed supply of antibody. As unlabelled hormone concentration rises, it displaces labelled antigen, reducing the radioactive signal — allowing quantification at femtomolar concentrations. RIA made it possible to measure circulating insulin, thyroid hormones, and cortisol for the first time, transforming endocrinology from a discipline of clinical inference into one of direct biochemical measurement. Modern enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and chemiluminescent immunoassay (CLIA) replace radioactivity with enzyme-linked or fluorescent reporters, achieving comparable sensitivity without radiation.
entries.note: Yalow, R. S. & Berson, S. A. (1959). 'Assay of plasma insulin in human subjects by immunological methods.' Nature, 184, 1648–1649 — the founding paper. Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1977 (Yalow; Berson died 1972 and was ineligible posthumously). Impact: the ability to measure plasma insulin concentrations revolutionised understanding of type 2 diabetes, hypoglycaemia, and insulinoma. ELISA: Engvall, E. & Perlmann, P. (1971). Immunochemistry, 8(9), 871–874; Van Weemen, B. K. & Schuurs, A. H. W. M. (1971). FEBS Letters, 15(3), 232–236 — independent co-inventions. Modern clinical immunoassay is automated, uses sandwich formats (two antibodies recognising different epitopes), and achieves attomolar sensitivity for some analytes. Cross-reactivity between related hormones (e.g., LH and FSH in older RIA formats) was an important early limitation; modern monoclonal-antibody-based assays are more specific.

entries.label: Micropuncture and Isolated Tubule Perfusion
entries.hint: Direct micropuncture of individual nephron tubules — using a glass micropipette visible through a stereomicroscope in a surgically exposed kidney — allows collection of nanolitre volumes of tubular fluid at defined nephron segments. Comparison with filtered plasma establishes fractional reabsorption and secretion of specific solutes at each site. In vitro microperfusion of isolated tubule segments adds controlled luminal and peritubular composition, defining transporter kinetics and regulation. Together these techniques established the distinct transport functions of the proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct that underlie all modern nephron physiology.
entries.note: Richards, A. N. (1924). Harvey Lectures — demonstrated that glomerular filtrate contains no protein and is isosmotic with plasma. Richards, A. N. (1938). 'Processes of urine formation.' Croonian Lecture, Proceedings of the Royal Society B, 126(843), 398–432 — comprehensive micropuncture characterisation. In vitro microperfusion: Burg, M., Grantham, J., Abramow, M. & Orloff, J. (1966). 'Preparation and study of fragments of single rabbit nephron segments in vitro.' American Journal of Physiology, 210(6), 1293–1298 — founded the isolated perfused tubule technique. Transporter identification enabled by these methods: SGLT2 (glucose): Koepsell, H., Lips, K. & Volk, C. (2007). Pharmaceutical Research, 24(7), 1227–1251; NHE3 (Na⁺/H⁺ exchanger), NKCC2 (Henle limb Na⁺-K⁺-Cl⁻ cotransporter), ENaC (collecting duct Na⁺ channel). Clinical translation: SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins) are a direct pharmaceutical offspring of micropuncture-defined glucose reabsorption kinetics.

categories.label: Pioneers & Theorists
categories.subtitle: The researchers who shaped the discipline

entries.label: William Harvey
entries.hint: Harvey (1578–1657) revolutionised medicine by proving, through quantitative argument and systematic animal dissection, that blood circulates continuously in a closed loop rather than being perpetually produced by the liver and consumed by tissues. His quantitative estimate that the heart ejects more blood per hour than the body contains made perpetual production physically impossible, and his ligature and valve dissection experiments demonstrated the directionality of flow. De Motu Cordis (1628) is the founding document of cardiovascular physiology and the first great achievement of experimental physiology as a discipline.
entries.note: Harvey, W. (1628). Exercitatio Anatomica de Motu Cordis et Sanguinis in Animalibus. W. Fitzer, Frankfurt. Harvey studied at Padua under Hieronymus Fabricius, who had described venous valves (without understanding their circulatory significance). Harvey was physician to King James I and Charles I. The capillary connection he postulated but could not visualise was confirmed by Marcello Malpighi (1661) using early microscopy. Harvey's willingness to contradict Galen — the unquestioned authority of 1,400 years — through empirical argument defined the experimental method for the scientific revolution in biology. Keynes, G. (1966). The Life of William Harvey. Oxford University Press — definitive biography.

entries.label: August Krogh
entries.hint: Krogh (1874–1949) demonstrated that capillary recruitment — opening of previously closed capillaries — is the primary mechanism by which skeletal muscle perfusion rises during exercise, not merely increased velocity in existing channels. His 'Krogh cylinder' model of tissue oxygenation provided the first quantitative geometry of O₂ delivery from capillary to surrounding tissue and established the relationship between capillary density and maximal O₂ uptake. He also designed the Krogh spirometer for measuring respiratory gases and contributed to the quantitative description of gas exchange in the lung.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1920. Krogh, A. (1922). The Anatomy and Physiology of Capillaries. Yale University Press. Krogh's career companion and wife Marie Krogh also contributed important work on CO₂ diffusion in the lung and was nominated for the Nobel Prize in her own right. The Krogh cylinder model was the dominant tool for tissue oxygenation calculations throughout the 20th century; it is now extended by computational microvascular network models (Secomb, T. W. 2017. Annual Review of Fluid Mechanics, 49, 443–461) that account for network topology and transit-time heterogeneity. Krogh's insight that microvascular architecture limits maximal O₂ uptake is foundational to exercise physiology and the training science of endurance athletes.

entries.label: Ernest Henry Starling
entries.hint: Starling (1866–1927) made two of the most clinically important discoveries in cardiovascular physiology. His 1896 paper established the Starling-Landis forces governing fluid movement between capillaries and tissues — the framework for understanding oedema, lymphatic function, and fluid therapy. His 1914 heart-lung preparation, conducted with Patterson and Piper, gave quantitative demonstration of the Frank-Starling law, showing that stroke volume is intrinsically regulated by ventricular filling. Starling also named and described the concept of 'hormones' in 1905 following Bayliss and Starling's discovery of secretin.
entries.note: Starling, E. H. (1896). 'On the absorption of fluids from the connective tissue spaces.' Journal of Physiology, 19(4), 312–326. Starling, E. H. (1918). 'The Linacre Lecture on the Law of the Heart.' Macmillan. Bayliss, W. M. & Starling, E. H. (1902). 'The mechanism of pancreatic secretion.' Journal of Physiology, 28(3), 325–353 — discovery of secretin, the first hormone. Starling coined the term 'hormone' (from Greek ὁρμάω, to set in motion) in his 1905 Croonian Lecture. Henderson, Y. & Prince, A. L. (1914). American Journal of Physiology, 35(1), 106–116 — Starling's influence on understanding circulatory shock. Starling's breadth — capillary physiology, cardiac mechanics, and endocrine physiology — was exceptional; he is one of the most influential British physiologists of the 20th century.

entries.label: Alan Lloyd Hodgkin
entries.hint: Hodgkin (1914–1998) developed the voltage-clamp technique and, with Andrew Huxley, produced the quantitative model of the action potential that bears their names. Working on the squid giant axon at Plymouth in 1939 and in the 1948–1952 series of experiments, they separated the ionic currents underlying the action potential into sodium and potassium components and encoded their dynamics in a set of differential equations that accurately predict every measured property of neural excitation. The Hodgkin-Huxley model remains the cornerstone of computational neuroscience and membrane biophysics.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1963 (shared with Huxley and Eccles). Hodgkin, A. L. (1964). The Conduction of the Nervous Impulse. Liverpool University Press — the Nobel Lecture and surrounding synthesis. Hodgkin, A. L. (1977). Chance and Design: Reminiscences of Science in Peace and War. Cambridge University Press — autobiography. Before the war, Hodgkin had already demonstrated that the extracellular ionic concentration changed the action potential amplitude, pointing toward an ionic basis: Hodgkin, A. L. & Huxley, A. F. (1939). 'Action potentials recorded from inside a nerve fibre.' Nature, 144, 710–711 — first intracellular recording. Post-war collaboration with Huxley produced the five-paper 1952 series. Hodgkin later served as President of the Royal Society (1970–1975) and Master of Trinity College Cambridge.

entries.label: Andrew Fielding Huxley
entries.hint: Andrew Huxley (1917–2012) made foundational contributions to two of the most important discoveries in 20th-century physiology. With Hodgkin he co-developed the voltage-clamp technique and the Hodgkin-Huxley model of the action potential (1952). With Jean Hanson he simultaneously demonstrated the sliding filament mechanism of muscle contraction (1954), using interference microscopy to show that filament lengths remain constant during shortening. He later developed the cross-bridge cycle theory (1957) and built a differential analyser to numerically integrate the Hodgkin-Huxley equations — by hand, before computers were available.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1963 (shared with Hodgkin and Eccles). Huxley, A. F. & Niedergerke, R. (1954). 'Structural changes in muscle during contraction.' Nature, 173, 971–973 — simultaneous with Huxley & Hanson (1954) in the same issue. Huxley, A. F. (1957). 'Muscle structure and theories of contraction.' Progress in Biophysics and Biophysical Chemistry, 7, 255–318 — the cross-bridge cycle formalism. Huxley, A. F. & Simmons, R. M. (1971). 'Proposed mechanism of force generation in striated muscle.' Nature, 233, 533–538 — quick-release experiments constraining the power stroke geometry. Andrew Huxley was the grandson of biologist Thomas Henry Huxley (Darwin's bulldog) and half-brother of novelist Aldous Huxley. Nobel Biography: Huxley, A. F. (1963). 'Nobel Lecture: The quantitative analysis of excitation and conduction in nerve.' Nobel Foundation.

entries.label: John Scott Haldane
entries.hint: J. S. Haldane (1860–1936) established CO₂ as the primary stimulus to ventilation, quantified the chemical composition of alveolar air, described the Haldane effect of deoxygenation on CO₂ carriage, and studied adaptations to high altitude, carbon monoxide poisoning, and decompression sickness. He developed the first practical gas analysis methods for measuring O₂ and CO₂ in expired air, personally volunteered for physiologically dangerous experiments (breathing CO-enriched air to characterise poisoning), and wrote Respiration (1922), the standard textbook for a generation.
entries.note: Haldane, J. S. & Priestley, J. G. (1905). 'The regulation of the lung-ventilation.' Journal of Physiology, 32(3–4), 225–266. Christiansen, J., Douglas, C. G. & Haldane, J. S. (1914). Journal of Physiology, 48(4), 244–271 — Haldane effect on CO₂ carriage. High altitude: Haldane, J. S., Kellas, A. M. & Kennaway, E. L. (1919). 'Experiments on acclimatisation to reduced atmospheric pressure.' Journal of Physiology, 53(3–4), 181–206. Haldane, J. S. & Priestley, J. G. (1935). Respiration, 2nd ed. Clarendon Press, Oxford. Haldane was the father of J. B. S. Haldane (the geneticist), a persistent source of confusion. He held no professorship at Oxford (never pursued an academic salary, working instead from a private laboratory at his home) and received no Nobel Prize, despite work that shaped the field for 50 years.

entries.label: Arthur Clifton Guyton
entries.loc: University of Mississippi Medical Center, Jackson, Mississippi
entries.hint: Guyton (1919–2003) constructed the first comprehensive mathematical model of the cardiovascular-renal system, asserting that long-term blood pressure is controlled primarily by the kidney's capacity to regulate blood volume rather than by vascular tone alone. His venous return / cardiac function curve framework showed that cardiac output is ultimately determined by the periphery (venous return) rather than the heart, reversing the traditional cardiocentric view. His textbook — Medical Physiology — became the world's best-selling physiology textbook, translated into over 13 languages.
entries.note: Guyton, A. C. (1955). 'Determination of cardiac output by equating venous return curves with cardiac response curves.' Physiological Reviews, 35(1), 123–129. Guyton, A. C. (1991). 'Blood pressure control — special role of the kidneys and body fluids.' Science, 252(5014), 1813–1816. Guyton, A. C. & Hall, J. E. (2015). Textbook of Medical Physiology, 13th ed. Elsevier — the current edition of the world's most widely used physiology textbook. Guyton suffered severe polio in 1946, was partially paralysed, and used a wheelchair for the rest of his career; he continued to conduct research and mentor students productively for five decades from Jackson, Mississippi. His renal-pressure natriuresis hypothesis — that the kidney has an infinite-gain feedback loop making it the obligatory long-term blood pressure regulator — remains the dominant framework in hypertension research, though challenged by models emphasising central neural contributions.

entries.label: John Burnham West
entries.loc: University of California San Diego, La Jolla, California
entries.hint: West (1928–2021) provided the definitive quantitative description of ventilation-perfusion inequality in the lung, developed the multiple inert gas elimination technique (MIGET) to resolve the full V̇/Q̇ distribution non-invasively, and systematically characterised the physiology of the lung at altitude — including leading a physiological expedition to the Himalayas in 1981 and measuring alveolar gas on the summit of Everest. His textbooks Respiratory Physiology and Pulmonary Pathophysiology have trained generations of clinicians and researchers worldwide.
entries.note: Riley, R. L. & Cournand, A. (1951). — West trained under Cournand in New York. Wagner, P. D., Saltzman, H. A. & West, J. B. (1974). 'Measurement of continuous distributions of ventilation-perfusion ratios.' Journal of Applied Physiology, 36(5), 588–599 — MIGET development. West, J. B. et al. (1983). 'Pulmonary gas exchange on the summit of Mt. Everest.' Journal of Applied Physiology, 55(3), 678–687. West, J. B. (2012). Respiratory Physiology: The Essentials, 9th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins. West, J. B. (2016). Pulmonary Pathophysiology: The Essentials, 9th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins. West was born in Adelaide, Australia, trained in London, and spent most of his career at UCSD. He received the Physiology or Medicine Prize from the International Society for Breathlessness (2017) and continued publishing until shortly before his death at 92.

entries.label: Homer William Smith
entries.loc: New York University School of Medicine, New York City, New York
entries.hint: Smith (1895–1962) created the conceptual and experimental framework of renal clearance physiology, establishing inulin clearance as the measure of glomerular filtration rate and para-aminohippurate clearance as the measure of renal plasma flow, allowing calculation of filtration fraction and tubular secretion for any substance. His monograph The Kidney (1951) synthesised half a century of renal physiology into a coherent quantitative framework that is still used. Smith was also a lifelong student of natural history and philosophy and wrote Kamongo (1932) — a popular novel about lungfish and evolution.
entries.note: Smith, H. W. (1951). The Kidney: Structure and Function in Health and Disease. Oxford University Press. Smith, H. W. (1956). Principles of Renal Physiology. Oxford University Press. Smith, H. W., Goldring, W. & Chasis, H. (1938). Journal of Clinical Investigation, 17(3), 263–278 — the landmark clearance paper. Smith narrowly missed a Nobel Prize; his work provided the experimental basis for renal pharmacology. Smith's evolutionary perspective — he traced the history of the vertebrate kidney from freshwater fish through terrestrial animals — gave him an integrative view of renal function that shaped his approach to experimental design. Shannon, J. A. (colleague, personal tribute, 1962): 'He was possessed of an unusual capacity for synthesis, for the reduction of a mass of experimental data to a simple, coherent whole.'

entries.label: Frederick Grant Banting
entries.hint: Banting (1891–1941) conceived and led the 1921–1922 experiments that extracted, purified, and demonstrated the therapeutic efficacy of insulin, transforming type 1 diabetes from a rapidly fatal disease into a manageable chronic condition. Working with Charles Best in J.J.R. Macleod's laboratory, Banting devised the pancreatic duct ligation strategy to suppress exocrine cells while preserving islet tissue, enabling extraction of adequate quantities of insulin for the first human trial in January 1922. The Nobel Prize awarded to Banting and Macleod (not Best or Collip) triggered a fierce credit dispute that has continued ever since.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1923 (Banting and Macleod). Banting, F. G. & Best, C. H. (1922). 'The internal secretion of the pancreas.' Journal of Laboratory and Clinical Medicine, 7(5), 251–266. Banting shared his prize money with Best; Macleod shared with Collip. Bliss, M. (1982). The Discovery of Insulin. University of Chicago Press — the definitive historical account, based on diaries and lab notebooks that reveal the bitter priority disputes. Banting was killed in a plane crash in Newfoundland in 1941 while on a wartime mission. Insulin structure: Sanger, F. (Nobel 1958) determined the full amino acid sequence of insulin — the first protein to be sequenced. Synthetic human insulin produced using recombinant DNA: Johnson, I. S. (1983). Science, 219(4585), 632–637 (Genentech/Eli Lilly).

entries.label: Earl Wilbur Sutherland Jr.
entries.loc: Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio
entries.hint: Sutherland (1915–1974) discovered cyclic AMP and established the second messenger concept — the principle that extracellular hormones bind surface receptors that generate an intracellular signal (the second messenger) which relays and amplifies the hormonal message without the hormone entering the cell. Working on adrenaline's activation of hepatic glycogen phosphorylase, he demonstrated that the hormonally stimulated intracellular factor was a heat-stable, acid-labile nucleotide he identified as cAMP. This framework transformed endocrinology from descriptive to mechanistic and created the basis for understanding G-protein coupled receptor signalling.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1971. Sutherland, E. W. & Rall, T. W. (1958). 'Fractionation and characterization of a cyclic adenine ribonucleotide formed by tissue particles.' Journal of Biological Chemistry, 232(2), 1077–1091. Sutherland, E. W. (1972). 'Studies on the mechanism of hormone action.' Science, 177(4047), 401–408 — Nobel Lecture. G-protein coupling of receptors to adenylyl cyclase was characterised by Rodbell, M. & Gilman, A. G. (Nobel 1994). Sutherland worked in Carl Cori's laboratory at Washington University before establishing his own group, and thus had firsthand knowledge of the biochemistry of glycogen metabolism. The second messenger framework was extended by Berridge and Irvine (IP₃/DAG pathway, 1984) and by Rasmussen (Ca²⁺ as second messenger), creating an integrated network of intracellular signalling cascades.

entries.label: Claude Bernard
entries.hint: Bernard (1813–1878) was the founder of modern experimental physiology and the originator of the concept of the milieu intérieur — the stable internal environment that higher animals maintain to free themselves from direct dependence on the external world. Working at the Collège de France and the Sorbonne, he discovered glycogen synthesis in the liver, demonstrated the vasomotor function of the sympathetic nervous system, identified the role of the pancreatic secretion in fat digestion, and showed that curare acts at the neuromuscular junction. Introduction à l'étude de la médecine expérimentale (1865) defined the experimental method in biology.
entries.note: Bernard, C. (1865). Introduction à l'étude de la médecine expérimentale. J.-B. Baillière, Paris — the philosophical and methodological manifesto. Bernard, C. (1878). Leçons sur les phénomènes de la vie communs aux animaux et aux végétaux. J.-B. Baillière — the developed account of milieu intérieur. Holmes, F. L. (1974). Claude Bernard and Animal Chemistry. Harvard University Press — detailed analysis of his laboratory practice. Bernard suffered serious domestic difficulties: his wife and daughters were militant anti-vivisectionists who opposed his experimental work and eventually separated from him. He was elected to both the Académie des sciences and the Académie française (unusually, as a scientist). On his death in 1878 he received a state funeral — the first French scientist to be honoured this way.

entries.label: Walter Bradford Cannon
entries.hint: Cannon (1871–1945) coined the term 'homeostasis', articulated its physiological mechanisms (negative feedback, antagonistic hormones, coordinated autonomic responses), described the fight-or-flight response and identified adrenaline as its primary humoral mediator, and championed the concept that the sympathetic nervous system acts as an emergency response system. The Wisdom of the Body (1932) translated Bernard's milieu intérieur into a coherent mechanistic account that shaped all subsequent integrative physiology. He also developed the X-ray fluoroscopy method for studying GI motility.
entries.note: Cannon, W. B. (1929). 'Organization for physiological homeostasis.' Physiological Reviews, 9(3), 399–431 — coined 'homeostasis.' Cannon, W. B. (1932). The Wisdom of the Body. W. W. Norton. Cannon, W. B. (1915). Bodily Changes in Pain, Hunger, Fear and Rage. D. Appleton & Co — fight-or-flight. Cannon, W. B. & de la Paz, D. (1911). 'Emotional stimulation of adrenal secretion.' American Journal of Physiology, 28(1), 64–70. Benison, S., Barger, A. C. & Wolfe, E. L. (1987). Walter B. Cannon: The Life and Times of a Young Scientist. Harvard University Press. Cannon was a lifelong activist: he served as a military physician in WWI, opposed McCarthyism, and supported Republican Spain. His wife Cornelia James Cannon was a novelist and social activist. His concept of homeostasis was the starting point for cybernetics (Wiener, N. 1948. Cybernetics) and systems biology.

entries.label: Geoffrey Wingfield Harris
entries.hint: Harris (1913–1971) established the neuroendocrine hypothesis: that the hypothalamus controls anterior pituitary function not by direct nerve connections but through releasing hormones secreted into the hypothalamo-pituitary portal blood. This proposed that neural signals (from higher brain centres, stress, circadian clocks) are transduced into hormonal signals at the portal capillaries, integrating the nervous and endocrine systems. Harris spent over two decades building the evidence for this hypothesis before Guillemin and Schally chemically isolated TRH and GnRH, confirming it — but he died in 1971, the year before the Nobel was awarded.
entries.note: Harris, G. W. (1955). Neural Control of the Pituitary Gland. Edward Arnold — the definitive monograph. Green, J. D. & Harris, G. W. (1947). 'The neurovascular link between the neurohypophysis and adenohypophysis.' Journal of Endocrinology, 5(2), 136–146 — anatomical demonstration of the portal system. Guillemin, R. & Schally, A. V. shared the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1977 — awarded for isolating the releasing hormones Harris had predicted. Harris died in 1971, aged 57, before the chemical confirmation was complete. The Nobel Prize is not awarded posthumously; this case is frequently cited in discussions of priority and recognition in science. Donovan, B. T. (1988). Hormones and Human Behaviour. Cambridge University Press — contextualises Harris's influence on neuroendocrinology.

entries.label: Ivan Petrovich Pavlov
entries.loc: Institute of Experimental Medicine, St Petersburg, Russia
entries.hint: Pavlov (1849–1936) received the 1904 Nobel Prize for his systematic investigation of the digestive glands — the first Nobel awarded to a physiologist in the strict sense. His chronic fistula preparations and 'sham feeding' experiments in dogs established the cephalic, gastric, and intestinal phases of gastric acid secretion, and the coordinating roles of the vagus nerve and humoral factors. He subsequently applied the same rigorous experimental approach to show that salivary and gastric reflexes could be transferred to arbitrary neutral stimuli through association, founding the science of classical conditioning and influencing all subsequent learning theory.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1904. Pavlov, I. P. (1902). The Work of the Digestive Glands. Griffin, London. Pavlov, I. P. (1927). Conditioned Reflexes: An Investigation of the Physiological Activity of the Cerebral Cortex. Oxford University Press — the conditioning synthesis. Babkin, B. P. (1949). Pavlov: A Biography. University of Chicago Press. Pavlov worked at the Institute of Experimental Medicine for 45 years, constructing elaborate laboratories and a dedicated animal colony. During the Russian Revolution and subsequent Soviet period, he was allowed to continue research unimpeded, though he openly criticised the Soviet government. His laboratory trained many prominent physiologists. The conditioning reflex arc — stimulus → cortical association → reflex response — became the foundation of behaviourism (Watson) and operant conditioning (Skinner), and remains influential in computational models of reinforcement learning.

entries.label: Karl Landsteiner
entries.hint: Landsteiner (1868–1943) discovered the ABO blood group system in 1901, demonstrating that mixing blood from individuals with incompatible types caused agglutination, and correctly inferring the existence of antigens on red blood cells and complementary antibodies in plasma. This discovery made safe blood transfusion possible and transformed surgery and emergency medicine. He subsequently co-discovered the Rh factor (1937, with Wiener), isolated the poliovirus (1908, with Popper), and established the hapten concept in immunology, showing that small organic molecules become antigenic when conjugated to proteins.
entries.note: Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine, 1930. Landsteiner, K. (1901). 'Ueber Agglutinationserscheinungen normalen menschlichen Blutes.' Wiener Klinische Wochenschrift, 14, 1132–1134 — ABO discovery. AB group: von Decastello, A. & Stürli, A. (1902). Münchener Medizinische Wochenschrift, 49, 1090–1095. Rh factor: Landsteiner, K. & Wiener, A. S. (1940). 'An agglutinable factor in human blood recognizable by immune sera for Rhesus blood.' Proceedings of the Society for Experimental Biology and Medicine, 43(1), 223. Rh incompatibility and haemolytic disease of the newborn: Levine, P. et al. (1941). American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology, 42(5), 925–937. Landsteiner moved to the Rockefeller Institute, New York in 1923, where he remained until his death; he died at his laboratory bench after a myocardial infarction at age 75. His work is the direct basis of pre-transfusion blood-bank compatibility testing, organ transplant crossmatching, and forensic blood-typing.

categories.label: Debates & Open Questions
categories.subtitle: Live controversies — where the field is actively contesting the evidence

entries.label: Homeostasis vs. Allostasis: Set-Points or Predictive Regulation?
entries.loc: University of California San Francisco, San Francisco, California
entries.hint: Classical homeostasis holds that physiological variables (blood pressure, core temperature, blood glucose) are regulated around fixed set-points by negative feedback. Allostasis — proposed by Sterling and Eyer in 1988 — argues that the regulated state is not fixed: it shifts continuously in anticipation of demand, driven by central neural predictive models rather than passive reactive feedback. The brain models the body's needs and proactively adjusts physiology before deviation occurs. McEwen extended allostasis to argue that chronic stress causes allostatic load — cumulative biological wear that is measurable and predicts disease. Critics respond that allostasis is homeostasis by another name, or that it conflates regulation with behaviour.
entries.note: Sterling, P. & Eyer, J. (1988). 'Allostasis: A new paradigm to explain arousal pathology.' In Fisher, S. & Reason, J. (Eds.), Handbook of Life Stress, Cognition and Health. Wiley, 629–649 — the founding paper, framing allostasis as a challenge to setpoint models. McEwen, B. S. & Stellar, E. (1993). 'Stress and the individual.' Archives of Internal Medicine, 153(18), 2093–2101 — allostatic load as predictive disease marker. McEwen, B. S. (1998). 'Stress, adaptation, and disease.' Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences, 840, 33–44 — quantifiable biomarkers of allostatic load. Critiques: Carpenter, R. H. S. (2004). 'Homeostasis: A plea for a unified approach.' Advances in Physiology Education, 28(4), 180–187 — argues allostasis is conceptually unnecessary. Ramsay, D. S. & Woods, S. C. (2014). 'Clarifying the roles of homeostasis and allostasis in physiological regulation.' Psychological Review, 121(2), 225–247 — proposes that homeostasis is error-correcting (after the fact) while allostasis is error-predicting (anticipatory), and both are operative.

entries.label: Glycocalyx Revision of Starling Forces: Does Reabsorption Occur?
entries.loc: Rutgers University, New Brunswick, New Jersey
entries.hint: The classical Starling-Landis model predicts that capillaries both filter (at the arteriolar end, where hydrostatic pressure exceeds oncotic) and reabsorb (at the venular end, where oncotic pressure exceeds hydrostatic). The glycocalyx revision — developed by Weinbaum, Tarbell, and Adamson from the 2000s — proposes that the primary oncotic barrier is not the capillary wall itself but the thin endothelial glycocalyx layer covering it, meaning that oncotic pressure acts across a sub-glycocalyx space rather than across the full lumen-to-interstitium gradient. In this revised model, there is no net reabsorption in venular capillaries under normal conditions; all filtered fluid returns via lymphatics. Crucially, because the glycocalyx acts as the primary oncotic barrier, the oncotic pressure of the bulk interstitial fluid has negligible influence on net filtration under steady-state conditions — interstitial oncotic pressure cannot ‘see across’ the glycocalyx to affect the sub-glycocalyx gradient. This explains why continuous, low-level filtration proceeds along the entire capillary length rather than being reversed at the venular end.
entries.note: Classic model: Landis, E. M. (1927). American Journal of Physiology, 82, 217–238. Glycocalyx challenge: Weinbaum, S., Tarbell, J. M. & Damiano, E. R. (2007). 'The structure and function of the endothelial glycocalyx layer.' Annual Review of Biomedical Engineering, 9, 121–167. Adamson, R. H. et al. (2004). 'Oncotic pressures opposing filtration across non-fenestrated rat microvessels.' Journal of Physiology, 557(3), 889–907 — experimental evidence in support of the revised Starling principle. Status: The glycocalyx model is increasingly accepted in specialist microvascular biology but has not fully displaced the classic Starling model in most clinical and teaching contexts. Practical implication: if the classic model overstated capillary reabsorption, the lymphatic system's role in tissue fluid homeostasis is greater than traditionally taught. Woodcock, T. E. & Woodcock, T. M. (2012). British Journal of Anaesthesia, 108(3), 384–394 — clinical implications for fluid therapy.

entries.label: Cardiac Output: Heart-Driven or Periphery-Driven?
entries.loc: McGill University Health Centre, Montreal, Quebec
entries.hint: Guyton's venous return model holds that cardiac output is ultimately determined by the periphery — by mean systemic filling pressure and the resistance to venous return — not by the intrinsic pumping capacity of the heart. The heart simply pumps whatever the periphery returns to it; cardiac output equals venous return at equilibrium. Critics argue that mean systemic filling pressure is an artificial construct difficult to measure in vivo, and that the venous return formalism misleadingly implies the heart is a passive participant. The empirical question is whether peripheral vascular changes independently drive cardiac output changes or whether cardiac performance is the independent variable.
entries.note: Guyton, A. C. (1955). Physiological Reviews, 35(1), 123–129. Critical appraisal: Magder, S. (2006). 'Point: The classical Guyton view that mean systemic pressure, right atrial pressure, and venous resistance govern venous return is/is not correct.' Journal of Applied Physiology, 101(5), 1523–1525. Levy, M. N. (1979). 'The cardiac and vascular factors that determine systemic blood flow.' Circulation Research, 44(6), 739–747. Defence: Hall, J. E. (2004). 'The pioneering use of systems analysis to study cardiac output regulation.' American Journal of Physiology — Regulatory, Integrative and Comparative Physiology, 287(5), R1074–R1077. Current status: most physiologists accept the Guyton equilibrium point as a useful analytical tool while acknowledging that mean systemic filling pressure is difficult to isolate experimentally in the intact circulation; some argue that the heart and periphery co-determine output simultaneously, and the Guyton model implicitly assumes separation of timescales that may not hold.

entries.label: Hypoxic Drive in COPD: Clinical Risk vs. Physiological Myth?
entries.hint: A longstanding clinical teaching holds that patients with severe COPD rely on peripheral hypoxic drive (because CO₂ receptors are 'blunted' by chronic hypercapnia), and that giving high-flow supplemental O₂ removes this drive, causing dangerous hypoventilation. This view recommends 'controlled O₂ therapy' with target saturations of 88–92%. The opposite view is that withholding O₂ from a hypoxaemic patient causes more harm than good, and that the Haldane effect (O₂ displacing CO₂ from haemoglobin) not ventilatory suppression accounts for most O₂-induced hypercapnia.
entries.note: Classic hypoxic drive teaching: Flenley, D. C. (1978). 'Clinical hypoxia: Causes, consequences and correction.' Lancet, 1(8063), 542–546. Primary evidence for O₂-induced hypercapnia via Haldane effect: Aubier, M. et al. (1980). 'Effects of the administration of O₂ on ventilation and blood gases in patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease during acute respiratory failure.' American Review of Respiratory Disease, 122(5), 747–754 — found O₂ increased dead space (via HPV reversal + Haldane) more than suppressed ventilation. Robinson, T. D. et al. (2000). 'The role of hypoventilation and ventilation-perfusion redistribution in oxygen-induced hypercapnia during acute exacerbations of COPD.' American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine, 161(5), 1524–1529 — confirmed Haldane + HPV reversal as primary mechanisms. O'Driscoll, B. R. et al. (2017). 'BTS guideline for oxygen use in adults in healthcare and ambulatory settings.' Thorax, 72(Suppl 1), ii1–ii90 — current BTS guidance: target SpO₂ 88–92% remains recommended for at-risk COPD to prevent hypercapnia, regardless of mechanism.

entries.label: Inner Medullary Urine Concentration: Active or Passive?
entries.loc: University of Texas Southwestern, Dallas, Texas
entries.hint: Kuhn's countercurrent multiplier model (1942) explained urinary concentration in the outer medulla by active NaCl pumping in the thick ascending limb of Henle. The inner medulla — where urine reaches its maximum concentration (up to 1,200 mOsm/kg) — lacks a thick ascending limb with active transport. Kokko and Rector (1972) proposed a 'passive mechanism': urea and NaCl gradients from the outer medulla sufficiently concentrate tubular fluid by osmosis in the thin descending limb, and urea recycling from the collecting duct sustains the inner medullary gradient passively. However, measurements have repeatedly found the proposed gradients insufficient to account for the observed concentrating ability, and the controversy remains open.
entries.note: Kuhn, W. & Ryffel, K. (1942). Hoppe-Seyler's Zeitschrift für Physiologische Chemie, 276, 145–178 — outer medullary mechanism. Passive model: Kokko, J. P. & Rector, F. C. (1972). 'Countercurrent multiplication system without active transport in inner medulla.' Kidney International, 2(4), 214–223; Stephenson, J. L. (1972). 'Concentration of urine in a central core model of the renal counterflow system.' Kidney International, 2(2), 85–94. Experimental challenge: Layton, A. T. et al. (2004). 'Permeability criteria for the functioning of the passive concentrating mechanism.' American Journal of Physiology — Renal Physiology, 286(4), F791–F800 — mathematical modelling shows the passive mechanism cannot account for maximal concentrating ability at physiologically measured permeabilities. Alternative models: Pannabecker, T. L. & Dantzler, W. H. (2007). 'Three-dimensional functional reconstruction of inner medullary thin limbs of Henle's loop.' American Journal of Physiology — Renal Physiology, 292(1), F545–F552 — anatomically detailed 3D models of the inner medullary architecture. The mechanism remains one of the unresolved problems in renal physiology.

entries.label: Insulin Resistance: Signal Defect or Lipid Overload?
entries.hint: Two competing mechanistic accounts of skeletal muscle insulin resistance dominate the field. The classical model (Shulman, Dresner) holds that intramyocellular lipid accumulation and diacylglycerol activate PKCθ and other serine kinases that phosphorylate IRS-1, impairing downstream insulin receptor signalling, GLUT4 translocation, and glucose uptake. The mitochondrial dysfunction model proposes that impaired mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation causes lipid accumulation upstream, making lipotoxicity the consequence rather than the primary cause of mitochondrial impairment. A third model implicates ceramides rather than diacylglycerol as the proximate effector.
entries.note: Shulman, G. I. (2000). 'Cellular mechanisms of insulin resistance.' Journal of Clinical Investigation, 106(2), 171–176 — the DAG/PKCθ hypothesis. Dresner, A. et al. (1999). 'Effects of free fatty acids on glucose transport and IRS-1-associated phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase activity.' Journal of Clinical Investigation, 103(2), 253–259. Mitochondrial model: Petersen, K. F. et al. (2004). 'Impaired mitochondrial activity in the insulin-resistant offspring of patients with type 2 diabetes.' New England Journal of Medicine, 350(7), 664–671 — ³¹P MRS evidence of mitochondrial dysfunction. Ceramide model: Chavez, J. A. & Summers, S. A. (2012). 'A ceramide-centric view of insulin resistance.' Cell Metabolism, 15(5), 585–594. Hotamisligil, G. S. (2006). 'Inflammation and metabolic disorders.' Nature, 444, 860–867 — inflammatory cytokines as an additional upstream driver. Status: All three mechanisms (DAG/PKCθ, ceramide, mitochondrial) likely contribute in different cell types and disease contexts; the debate about primary driver vs. downstream effector is ongoing.

entries.label: Volutrauma vs. Barotrauma: What Injures the Ventilated Lung?
entries.loc: University of California San Francisco, San Francisco, California
entries.hint: Traditional ventilator practice prioritised preventing barotrauma — injury from excessive airway pressure. Dreyfuss and Saumon (1988) demonstrated in animal models that it is overdistension of the alveolus (high tidal volume, 'volutrauma') — not high pressure per se — that causes ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI): the same high airway pressure in a chest-strapped animal that limited lung volume caused no injury. The landmark ARDSNet RCT (2000) translated this into clinical practice with a 6 mL/kg ideal body weight lung-protective protocol that reduced ARDS mortality by 22% compared to 12 mL/kg, firmly establishing volutrauma as the primary injurious mechanism.
entries.note: Dreyfuss, D., Soler, P., Basset, G. & Saumon, G. (1988). 'High inflation pressure pulmonary edema: Respective effects of high airway pressure, high tidal volume, and positive end-expiratory pressure.' American Review of Respiratory Disease, 137(5), 1159–1164. Slutsky, A. S. (1993). 'Barotrauma and volutrauma during mechanical ventilation.' American Review of Respiratory Disease, 147(4), 1048–1049 — framed the conceptual distinction. ARDSNet (2000). 'Ventilation with lower tidal volumes as compared with traditional tidal volumes for acute lung injury and the acute respiratory distress syndrome.' New England Journal of Medicine, 342(18), 1301–1308 — the definitive RCT. Current frontier: Gattinoni, L. et al. (2010). 'Lung stress and strain during mechanical ventilation.' Intensive Care Medicine, 36(8), 1267–1274 — proposed that it is stress (transpulmonary pressure) and strain (ΔV/FRC) rather than tidal volume per se that determines injury; plateau pressure of ≤ 30 cmH₂O may be safer than a fixed tidal volume target.

entries.label: Sex and Gender in Physiology: How Deep Do Biological Differences Run?
entries.loc: University of California Los Angeles, Los Angeles, California
entries.hint: Physiology textbooks historically described organ function from male-default experimental data, and many physiological parameters — drug pharmacokinetics, cardiovascular risk thresholds, exercise capacity, respiratory reference values — have since been shown to differ systematically between biological sexes. Taylor (2000) proposed that the female stress response is qualitatively different from fight-or-flight: a 'tend-and-befriend' pattern driven by oxytocin and social affiliation rather than adrenaline and aggression. The deeper debate is whether sex-based differences reflect biological sex (chromosomal, gonadal, hormonal) or gender (sociocultural), and how to handle both in research design and clinical translation without essentialising or ignoring them.
entries.note: Taylor, S. E. et al. (2000). 'Biobehavioral responses to stress in females: Tend-and-befriend, not fight-or-flight.' Psychological Review, 107(3), 411–429. Institute of Medicine (2001). Exploring the Biological Contributions to Human Health: Does Sex Matter? National Academies Press — the comprehensive review that documented systematic exclusion of female subjects from biomedical research. NIH policy (2016): Hankenson, F. C. D. et al. (2018). 'Inclusion of sex as a biological variable.' ILAR Journal, 58(3), 371–377 — NIH now requires sex as a biological variable in preclinical grant applications. Zamponi, G. W. (2016). 'Sex differences in pain and analgesia.' Pain, 157(6), 1229–1230. Critique of overstating binary differences: Hyde, J. S. et al. (2019). 'The future of sex and gender in psychology: Five challenges to the gender binary.' American Psychologist, 74(2), 171–193 — argues that sex and gender exist on continua and that binary models obscure as much as they reveal.

categories.label: Applications
categories.subtitle: Where the science leaves the lab

entries.label: Mechanical Ventilation and Lung-Protective Strategies
entries.loc: ARDS Clinical Trials Network (Multi-site), United States
entries.hint: Mechanical ventilation replaces or augments the work of breathing by applying positive pressure to the airway. Contemporary lung-protective ventilation — motivated by the volutrauma debate — limits tidal volume to 6 mL/kg ideal body weight and plateau pressure to ≤ 30 cmH₂O, accepting 'permissive hypercapnia' rather than using larger tidal volumes to normalise PaCO₂. Additional strategies include prone positioning (redistributes perfusion, improves V̇/Q̇ matching in ARDS), recruitment manoeuvres, and optimised PEEP to prevent cyclical alveolar derecruitment. The ARDSNet trial (2000) reduced ARDS mortality from ~40% to ~31% with the 6 mL/kg protocol.
entries.note: ARDSNet (2000). New England Journal of Medicine, 342(18), 1301–1308. PROSEVA trial: Guérin, C. et al. (2013). 'Prone positioning in severe acute respiratory distress syndrome.' New England Journal of Medicine, 368(23), 2159–2168 — prone positioning reduced 28-day mortality from 32.8% to 16.0% in severe ARDS. CPAP/NIV: Sullivan, C. E. et al. (1981). 'Reversal of obstructive sleep apnoea by continuous positive airway pressure applied through the nares.' Lancet, 1(8225), 862–865 — invention of CPAP. COVID-19 experience (2020–2022) confirmed lung-protective ventilation principles across millions of patients and renewed debate about prone positioning thresholds and non-invasive ventilation in hypoxaemic respiratory failure. Diaphragm protective ventilation: Goligher, E. C. et al. (2018). 'Evolution of diaphragm thickness during mechanical ventilation.' American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine, 197(9), 1–10 — optimising patient effort to prevent ventilator-induced diaphragm dysfunction.

entries.label: RAAS Inhibition in Hypertension and Heart Failure
entries.loc: Squibb Institute for Medical Research, Princeton, New Jersey
entries.hint: The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) is the primary endocrine axis for long-term blood pressure regulation: renin (from juxtaglomerular cells in response to reduced renal perfusion or low Na⁺) cleaves angiotensinogen to angiotensin I, which ACE converts to angiotensin II — a potent vasoconstrictor and aldosterone secretagogue. ACE inhibitors (captopril, enalapril) block this final step; ARBs block angiotensin II receptors; MRAs block aldosterone at the kidney. RAAS inhibition is the cornerstone of treating hypertension, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, diabetic nephropathy, and post-myocardial infarction management.
entries.note: Discovery of renin: Tigerstedt, R. & Bergman, P. G. (1898). 'Niere und Kreislauf.' Skandinavisches Archiv für Physiologie, 8, 223–271. Captopril: Cushman, D. W. & Ondetti, M. A. (1978). Biochemical Pharmacology, 27(6), 843–851. CONSENSUS trial: CONSENSUS Trial Study Group (1987). 'Effects of enalapril on mortality in severe congestive heart failure.' New England Journal of Medicine, 316(23), 1429–1435 — first major heart failure mortality trial, 40% reduction in 6-month mortality. SOLVD: SOLVD Investigators (1991). NEJM, 325(5), 293–302. ARBs: CHARM (Granger, C. B. et al. 2003. NEJM, 349, 2657–2667). MRAs: RALES (Pitt, B. et al. 1999. NEJM, 341, 709–717) — spironolactone reduced mortality 30% in severe heart failure. Finerenone (non-steroidal MRA) added cardiorenal endpoint benefits in CKD: FIDELIO-DKD (Bakris, G. et al. 2020. NEJM, 383, 2219–2229).

entries.label: Renal Replacement Therapy and Dialysis
entries.hint: Dialysis replaces the glomerular filtration and solute-removal functions of the kidney by exposing blood (or peritoneal fluid) to a semipermeable membrane across which solutes diffuse down concentration gradients (haemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis) or are removed by convection (haemofiltration). The driving principles are the Gibbs-Donnan equilibrium and Fick diffusion: small solutes (urea, creatinine, K⁺) are removed efficiently, while larger molecules and protein-bound toxins are less effectively cleared. Timing, dose (Kt/V), and modality (intermittent vs. continuous) profoundly affect outcomes in acute kidney injury and end-stage renal disease.
entries.note: Kolff, W. J. & Berk, H. T. (1944). 'The artificial kidney: A dialyser with a great area.' Acta Medica Scandinavica, 117(2), 121–134 — first rotating drum artificial kidney; first successful human dialysis in 1943 during WWII in occupied Netherlands. Peritoneal dialysis: Ganter, G. (1923). Münchener Medizinische Wochenschrift, 70, 1478–1480 — first clinical peritoneal dialysis. Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT): Kramer, P. et al. (1977). Klinische Wochenschrift, 55(22), 1121–1122. KDIGO AKI guidelines (2012). Kidney International Supplements, 2(1), 1–138. IDEAL-ICU RCT (Barbar, S. M. et al. 2018. NEJM, 379, 1431–1442): delaying dialysis until a clear indication emerges did not increase mortality — timing is a major ongoing controversy. Home haemodialysis and incremental dialysis prescriptions aim to maintain residual kidney function as long as possible.

entries.label: SGLT2 Inhibitors: From Tubular Physiology to Cardiovascular Outcomes
entries.hint: Sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) in the early proximal tubule reabsorbs approximately 90% of filtered glucose, a function first characterised by micropuncture and isolated tubule perfusion. SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins: empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin) block this transport, causing glycosuria. The 2015 EMPA-REG OUTCOME trial revealed unexpectedly large reductions in cardiovascular mortality and heart failure hospitalisation — independent of glycaemic effects — surprising the field and establishing gliflozins as the first diabetes drug to reduce cardiovascular events. Subsequent trials confirmed similar benefits in heart failure and chronic kidney disease even in patients without diabetes.
entries.note: SGLT2 characterisation: Koepsell, H. (2020). 'Glucose transporters in the small intestine in health and disease.' Pflügers Archiv, 472(9), 1207–1248. EMPA-REG OUTCOME: Zinman, B. et al. (2015). 'Empagliflozin, cardiovascular outcomes, and mortality in type 2 diabetes.' New England Journal of Medicine, 373(22), 2117–2128 — 14% reduction in MACE, 38% reduction in hospitalisation for heart failure. DAPA-HF: McMurray, J. J. V. et al. (2019). 'Dapagliflozin in patients with heart failure and reduced ejection fraction.' NEJM, 381(21), 1995–2008 — benefit in heart failure regardless of diabetic status. DAPA-CKD: Heerspink, H. J. L. et al. (2020). NEJM, 383(15), 1436–1446 — CKD benefit. Proposed mechanisms: osmotic natriuresis → volume unloading; ketone body shift improving myocardial fuel efficiency; direct tubular and cardiomyocyte cytoprotective effects.

entries.label: Cardiac Pacemakers and Defibrillators
entries.loc: Montefiore Medical Center, New York City, New York
entries.hint: The cardiac pacemaker exploits the action potential physiology of cardiac myocytes: by delivering a suprathreshold electrical stimulus at the myocardium, it reliably initiates a propagated action potential and coordinated contraction. Modern pacing devices programme rate, atrioventricular delay, and sense-pace logic to mimic normal conduction. The implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) continuously monitors cardiac rhythm and delivers a high-energy shock (typically 30–40 joules) to terminate ventricular fibrillation, exploiting the principle that simultaneous depolarisation of the entire myocardium abolishes re-entrant circuits and allows spontaneous recovery of organised rhythm from the sino-atrial node.
entries.note: First implantable pacemaker: Elmqvist, R. & Senning, A. (1960). 'An implantable pacemaker for the heart.' In: Smyth, C. N. (Ed.), Medical Electronics: Proceedings of the 2nd International Conference on Medical Electronics. Iliffe & Sons, London, 253–254. Transvenous pacing: Furman, S. & Robinson, G. (1958). 'The use of an intracardiac pacemaker in the correction of total heart block.' Surgical Forum, 9, 245–248 — Montefiore Hospital. ICD: Mirowski, M. et al. (1980). 'Termination of malignant ventricular arrhythmias with an implanted automatic defibrillator in human beings.' New England Journal of Medicine, 303(6), 322–324. MADIT II: Moss, A. J. et al. (2002). 'Prophylactic implantation of a defibrillator in patients with myocardial infarction and reduced ejection fraction.' NEJM, 346(12), 877–883 — established ICD for primary prevention of SCD. Biventricular/CRT pacing: CARE-HF (2005) — cardiac resynchronisation therapy improved survival and quality of life in heart failure with bundle branch block.

entries.label: Hormonal Contraception and Reproductive Endocrinology
entries.loc: G. D. Searle & Company, Skokie, Illinois
entries.hint: Combined oral contraceptives containing synthetic oestrogen and progestogen suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis by providing sustained exogenous sex hormone levels that inhibit GnRH pulsatility, suppressing FSH and LH surges and preventing follicular development and ovulation. This is a direct pharmaceutical application of Harris's neuroendocrine hypothesis and the negative feedback architecture of the HPG axis. Modern formulations use lower oestrogen doses and diverse progestogens to minimise thrombotic and metabolic side effects while preserving efficacy. Progestogen-only methods and non-hormonal IUDs exploit different mechanisms.
entries.note: Pincus, G., Garcia, C. R. & Rock, J. (1959). 'Effects of certain 19-nor steroids on reproductive processes in normal women.' Science, 130(3371), 81–82. FDA approval of Enovid (norethynodrel + mestranol): June 23, 1960. Rock, J., Garcia, C. R. & Pincus, G. (1957). 'Synthetic progestins in the normal human menstrual cycle.' Recent Progress in Hormone Research, 13, 323–346. Margaret Sanger's influence: Pincus was approached by Sanger (via Katharine McCormick) who funded the research; the political and feminist context of the Pill's development is inseparable from its scientific history. GnRH agonists and antagonists as therapeutic tools: Knobil, E. (1980). 'The neuroendocrine control of the menstrual cycle.' Recent Progress in Hormone Research, 36, 53–88 — GnRH pulse frequency determines FSH vs. LH secretion, explaining how GnRH agonist downregulation is used in IVF protocols and prostate cancer treatment.

entries.label: Proton Pump Inhibitors (PPIs)
entries.hint: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole) irreversibly inhibit the gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase by forming a covalent disulphide bond with cysteine residues (Cys813, Cys822) after acid-catalysed activation of the benzimidazole prodrug in the acidic environment of parietal cell secretory canaliculi. This irreversible mechanism means acid suppression outlasts the drug's plasma half-life (~1–2 h) by 18–36 hours — recovery requires de novo pump synthesis. PPIs are among the most widely prescribed drugs worldwide, used for peptic ulcer disease, gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GERD), Zollinger-Ellison syndrome, and (combined with antibiotics) H. pylori eradication. Long-term concerns include increased risk of Clostridioides difficile infection, hypomagnesaemia (chronic Mg²⁺ malabsorption in the small intestine), and fractures (reduced Ca²⁺ absorption at low luminal pH).
entries.note: Synthesis: Berglindh, T. et al. (1979). 'H+/K+-ATPase in secreting oxyntic cells.' Acta Physiologica Scandinavica Supplementum, 473, 15–26 — first characterisation of the target. Omeprazole: Fellenius, E. et al. (1981). 'Substituted benzimidazoles inhibit gastric acid secretion by blocking (H⁺+K⁺)-ATPase.' Nature, 290, 159–161 — discovery paper. Lindberg, P. et al. (1990). 'Omeprazole: The first proton pump inhibitor.' Medicinal Research Reviews, 10(1), 1–54 — drug class review. FDA approval: 1989 (omeprazole/Prilosec). H. pylori eradication: Maastricht V/Florence Consensus (2017) recommends PPI-based triple or quadruple therapy for H. pylori eradication. Overuse controversy: Katz, M. H. (2010). 'Failing the acid test.' Archives of Internal Medicine, 170(9), 747–748 — approximately 40–70% of PPI prescriptions are without appropriate indication. Dementia association: observational data suggested a link; subsequent Mendelian randomisation studies found no causal effect (Gomm, W. et al. 2016 versus Lochhead, P. et al. 2021).

events.l: Haldane: CO₂ as primary ventilatory stimulus
O₂ therapy in COPD: Haldane effect vs. drive suppression
Volutrauma: tidal volume, not pressure, injures lung
Insulin resistance: DAG/PKCθ signalling defect proposed
Insulin: First human treatment of diabetes
PPIs approved: irreversible gastric acid suppression
EMPA-REG: SGLT2 inhibitors cut CV mortality
